sentences.text: Yeah.
What-- happened happened up to now?
OK.
[SIGHS] Shall I say during the time when we were occupied with the Russians or shall I skip before the-- before the-- you know.
As I-- OK, being at school, at the medical school, we heard in 1941 that it's a spectacle we're having.
Because we got-- were occupied by the Russians since 1939.
I mean, Poland was occupied by the Russians in 1939.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we-- we heard that a lot of trucks were sent from Russia to pick up the Jews who are willing to leave Poland to Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Many of them went.
Well, they did suffer.
Some of them suffered a lot.
Some of them were sent to Siberia and this and that.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was already pregnant, so I was afraid to go on trucks.
They say that trucks-- I was thinking to myself, and we remained in-- as the bombs were falling already.
We were living in [INAUDIBLE] of '45 in Rowne and-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] and the-- the Germans occupied us.
Didn't take too long.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We heard we have a Judenrat was-- was announced on-- on the post-- post.
The Jews cannot walk without the yellow Magen David star.
And the Jews are not allowed to walk on the street until at least they-- if they have jobs.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so we were in-- in the houses.
We-- we had a-- we started to get bad feelings, scaredness.
And they hear-- we-- anything what we want to know, we shall go to the Judenrat.
And every order with the Judenrat receives from the German government, they have to fulfill, and they will give the orders to the civil population.
And we are supposed to fulfill that.
Beg your pardon?
Not-- not in the-- not in the beginning.
We were like a few months, you know, sort of free but with the yellow stars.
And all of a sudden, we heard from-- from the Judenrat they came from-- that there is a Jewish police.
What consisted the Jewish police?
They gave them like two-- two sticks, heavy sticks.
And if any Jew want-- if they refused to obey the order, then they shall-- they have the right to hit him and bring them to the police.
Otherwise, they will be killed.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So it started.
First-- first of all, the men, they came from house to house and take all the young men to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you think the work consisted of?
To dig graves, to dig long graves where they will be able to-- to bury the Jews who will be killed.
But they didn't say that.
The Judenrat didn't know about that.

They had to fulfill and get those young men-- the men, doesn't matter-- young, old, mostly the young men, not young young, but you know like in the 20's and the 30's and even the 40's, you know, the real old ones, they still left by the end.
They never returned back.
They were taken to work.
They never heard from them, and at lease some of them were so strong and so careful that they ran away.
They ran away from there.
You see, they get-- got that feeling not to-- not to trust the Germans.
Most of those people who didn't trust them, those tried away how to hide themselves, how to run away.
As I was told when I was young, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can't trust everybody especial a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, who is-- who is not-- who is not a friend.
What is the opposite way?
Enemy.
Enemy.
[INAUDIBLE].
Don't trust the enemy.
Run away from him.
And this is what remained in my memory.
From the first time whatever-- OK.
In that time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in that time when we had those trunks to be able to go to Russia, Harry said he wants to see how his mother is.
He went back to Stavische, and I gave birth to the little baby.
Before he left, he told me if I'll-- if I'll give birth to-- there will be a boy, I shall name him after his father, [?
Olaf ?] [?
Asholam, ?] and this what I have done.
And I gave birth to the baby by closed windows in my house.
And before-- about a day or two days before, I went to the Jewish hospital.
And I talked to one of the doctors who he used to teach me.
And I said within a day or two, I-- I will have to, but I can't go to a hospital.
I have to give birth for-- to my baby in my house.
So he said, I'll send her.
She came twice, once was the orderly, the other one, she came, a Jewish, a mother, in my house.
But she said she's afraid the Germans shall not see us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I gave birth to a healthy child, and I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found out that a mohel and one rabbi are hidden in a-- not too far from us in a-- was like a-- a garden with trees.
And they are somewhere-- there is some like-- like one little shed.
In that shed, I can find him.
And this mohel happened [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before this happened, he was-- their house was not too far where we were living.
So I went with a child.
And they [INAUDIBLE] there.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believed due to the fact I was always thinking maybe God will help me because I am so devoted to the Jewish tradition, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but not fanatic, modern tradition, as up till now.
You know.
But now we became-- because we merged with a-- with an Orthodox synagogue.
But before was conservative.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I have the baby.
I didn't have no-- not sufficient milk or-- or anything what would give me, you know, milk.
I shall be able to provide natural from mother's breast to my baby.
So I-- I asked my landlady, and I said, what shall I do?
What shall I do?
Where shall I get sugar?
So she found out there-- as the Germans occupied us, the Germans including Banderas, Ukrainians, they robbed all the stores.

If anyone, any owner of the business could grab something to save in his pockets or-- or in his hands, then could be that this family [INAUDIBLE], which owned a pharmacy maybe they have saccharin or sugar, and they told me where they live in the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this is why I went, and I got from them the saccharin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that gave me-- there, that gave me-- was a miracle.
That gave me the first step to help me to my life, to my surviving.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
There was sitting near me, was sitting a man and he was waiting.
I came after him.
He was waiting for something which this lady will be bringing out for him.
And as I just-- he-- I was sitting near him, but I noticed that his jacket is sort of with paint, like he would be a painter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was already like a few months, maybe two months after being occupied by the Germans.
They gave out 2,000 [NON-ENGLISH] for more than 22,000 or more than 24,000 Jews in Rowne.
Rowne was a large city, long city and very well known.
I don't know on what rate, but this was a most maybe the third rate largest cities in Poland, maybe fourth.
But it was Warsaw, Krakow, Lodz, I think the fourth one, more or less.
A [NON-ENGLISH] yes.
OK.
On the [NON-ENGLISH], they only gave out the 2,000 [NON-ENGLISH] to those people who actually worked for them.
For the Germans.
Not to all of them.
Perhaps if there were much more working for them for nothing, but they just picked out the most, like a few doctors, a few-- a few painters, a few-- I don't know.
Altogether for that large population, was only giving out 2,000.
That person better survive or a woman or a man.
So if it's a man, his wife can be included.
It's written the same [NON-ENGLISH] because I read it.
And they can have included also children up to 14 years of age.
There-- those people who has the [NON-ENGLISH] will be called to work.
Those which doesn't have the [NON-ENGLISH] shall-- cannot walk on the street from this day from 5 o'clock on.
They shall stay in the house, in the afternoon, in the evening, in the house.
And from-- from 5 o'clock in the morning, they should leave everything in their houses, but not the most valuable items on their-- in their rucksacks.
They can take-- they have, you know, gold or-- or what-- with clothes or whatever with them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Happened when I went back, I'm going back to the-- by getting the saccharin.
This man, I think his name was Geller, [?
Shmuluk ?] Geller.
I wish I can find him sometimes if he is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He left before me, and after I received the-- I-- by going back, I read that sign that we are not allowed to but-- but those who have the [NON-ENGLISH].
So some kind of [? idea ?] when I came into the house after giving-- making a little bit of that milk for my child, I thought to myself, maybe I should go to the Judenrat.
It's still time.
That was maybe around 2 o'clock.
To see, maybe there are some men like from-- who has [NON-ENGLISH], but their wives went to Russia or their wife passed away, anything can happen.
So we-- I will promise to pay them.
They shall include me and my child, but it can't be happened, but I never give-- I am not giving up.
If I get an-- an idea, I have to try to work it out.
If I can, I can, but I have to try it even over and over.
I came-- so until I opened the door, when I opened the door, I saw my-- this landlady was standing near that oven.
You know, we used to have a European oven She was baking pletzlach, you know-- you know, pretzels.
You know, like to take it with us.
So she said, you know, where were you?
We have to be in the house.
Where were you?
We have to go 5 o'clock.
What I wrote also that-- when I escaped, the Germans fooled us, just to believe them.
It was also written that from-- we shall go all the way to the [NON-ENGLISH].
It means-- [NON-ENGLISH] is like small, young trees, many of them, but the end of the-- of Rowne.
Rowne was a long city.
We had to walk.
And there will be trucks, and we will be going to [?
Chuchen. ?] [?
Chuchen ?] was a small city, a small town.
And there will be a Juden Stadt.
Juden Stadt.
Stadt is a city, a Jewish city.
They didn't wanted us to think something different, so they-- they gave you lies and some people believed them.
When I saw the lady-- my-- coming into the house, and I saw my landlady was standing and-- and baking those little rolls or whatever.
So I said, what are you doing?
What are you baking it for?
She said, don't you know?
We have to-- we will be going to [?
Chuchen ?] and there will be a Jewish city.
What do you want?
I said, you believe it?
She said, she remembers that during the First World War, she was-- she and her parents were doing very good business with the Germans, but the First World War I.
So I said, but the time is different.
I don't believe.
Did you read it?
I said, yes, I read, and I'm telling you what you should do.
Let's look.
Maybe we can hide ourselves under the roof.
Let's try.
Maybe in the-- in the basement.
She said, oh, no.
Oh, no.
I don't believe that.
We will go with everybody.
All the Jews will be going.
So I thought to myself, I let out-- with a day before, a-- an old woman came into my house and she told me a story.
She came in with two grandchildren, one was Esther and the other one was-- I don't remember.
Her name was [?
Yanga. ?] She said maybe you can help me with a piece of bread or anything what you have.
So I said, OK.
And I looked at that Esther, and I said, where is-- are her parents?
She said, her son was killed by the bombs, just like walking, and he was killed.
And he had-- this is his children from his second wife, and she can't help herself.
So I said, if I'll tell you that Esther shall stay with me, will this help you?
She said, Esther, the-- you want to live with this woman?
She said, oh, yes, and she grabbed me, and she kissed me like my own, like my own child.
And then I-- I had something cooked which was prepared, whatever I had, I gave that older woman.
She ate it, and she-- and she-- and the older woman left Esther with me and she went with the other little girl.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was a few days before, did I tell you?
This was a few-- not in the same day, a few days before.
When she found out, this-- this grandma, what happened that the Jews will be going to [?
Chuchen, ?] to a city-- to a Jewish city, so she came all alone.
She said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, Mrs.
Cmielewski, my-- my step daughter-in-law told me to bring Esther with me.
She wants to have Esther going together, all of them together.
I almost-- I did cried.
I-- I fell in love with this child.
I thought to myself, whatever I do, I'll have another girl, so what.
And so then I talked to myself.
I took-- I had a coat, so I said wait a minute, my own coat, I made it shorter, bend it down with the needle.
Fast, fast, fast.
She shall have-- it was like Rosh Hashanah, you know, cold weathers.
And it was like November, November the 5th.
Yeah.
Was the first-- first snow falling and whatever I-- I could give.
Yeah, I made her a rucksack with some of my lingerie and they left.
So I remained with my baby boy.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this evening before, the same evening, I thought to myself, oh, the-- the same day, I said to Esther, I said, Esther, you want to go with me?
It was still day time.
She said where?
Where can we go?
It's almost 4 o'clock.
We're-- we have to be in the house by 5:00.
I said, we will make it.
So she went-- she went with me.
Where did we go?
To the Judenrat.
I'm telling you again where I let out.
I said-- she said, what is on your idea?
I said maybe we can still find a man who will-- who has a [NON-ENGLISH].
So she said, well, I'm going with you, I'm going with you and I'm holding Esther.

And as we-- we walked on one side of the street, like in the-- and-- and the Judenrat was-- the building-- the Judenrat was exactly the building where-- where I attended the school, the medical school.
I'll never forget that.
And so what did I see?
We wanted to make sure what is written of the-- people always, they hung up the sign with orders.
People shall know.
So what did I notice?
I notice a man in a dark jacket and the white paint on his back.
So I said, Esther, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I think I'm helped.
I got helped.
She said, Molly, [NON-ENGLISH].
I'm not-- I'm not crossing, and I'm not going around.
I'm going.
She ran home fast.
And I [? walked ?] so.
I wanted to make sure that's him.
Maybe somebody else has a-- and I-- as he was sending in a few more people, women and men.
And I was just-- I was lucky.
He was close to the edge reading.
And I said to mister-- hello.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You know, good evening or good afternoon, I don't-- whatever it was.
So he said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
How are you?
I said, do you remember me?
He said, we met just now, just maybe one hour at the [INAUDIBLE].
I said, I want to ask you a question.
He said, what is it?
I said, do you have a [NON-ENGLISH]?
Because I see the paint.
He said, yes.
I said, are you married?
He said, yes.
Do you have children?
I have a little child, a little baby boy.
I met her here.
I-- he's from [?
Clevon ?], he told me.
I should advertise maybe, you know, to look for him, just as an appreciation.
[NON-ENGLISH] I am alive.
And so he said, this woman-- I married this wife mine who was born in the Russia.
And as the trucks were waiting-- I'm going back to the trucks now.
So she decided to take the baby and go back because she was positive that it will be of war.
And he-- he received for drafting.
So she knew that he will be going to the-- to the army, so she went back-- she went back on those trucks home to Russia.
He doesn't know where.
So I said, Mr.
Geller, you know, in Russia, your-- your wife could-- name could be Geller.
My name is [?
Milevski. ?] Well, I can have my-- my passport and I can still be-- is written, the name of your wife?
He said, no, there is no names, just a wife and a child, which is-- So I said you know that I have a feeling that I am in your hands.
You can save two lives.
My husband is now in Russia.
He's a good man.
Could be that your wife is asking for help from my husband.
Can you imagine that?
I would never dream that I would be standing in front of a man to ask to help me, to save my life.
He says, I get chills, but I have to go to ask my mother.
His mother was living with him.
So I said, OK.
I'll go with you.
The child was there with my, you know, landlady when I left.
So I went.
He was before, and she was living in the main street, [INAUDIBLE].
And she walked in-- and she-- and he walks in and she say, who is that?
Who is after you?
He said, Mother, it's a history that you will-- I just wanted to ask you.
You shall tell me yes or not.
That's it.
And he told the story.
She said, oy, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
It's out of control.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] If they will find us a strange woman, you don't have no [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, don't worry about that.
Please let me-- let me come.
I have a baby [INAUDIBLE].
So she said, go home.
We will be going.
I-- she had one son, what I found out and one-- one was the-- the little main landlady's from the building.
It was a large property.
Her daughter was under the roof.
So she said to me, it's very dangerous.
You will have to be responsible for them.
And in case what, they will kill you too.
All around.
Go take your baby and come with us where all the Jews are going.
So I thought to myself, but I cannot see any danger-- more danger than-- than this.
It's danger to go to the [NON-ENGLISH], but here dangerous.
OK, we have to stop.
